parentQuestion: When we move from darkness to bright light, retinal sensitivity is lost, but visual acuity is gained.

scaffolds.q: In darkness, RODS are the primary active photoreceptors.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Rods are exquisitely sensitive - can detect single photons.

scaffolds.q: In bright light, CONES become the primary active photoreceptors.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Cones need more light but provide color and sharp detail.

scaffolds.q: Rods have HIGH sensitivity but LOW resolution (spatial acuity).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! High convergence = detect dim light, but coarse detail.

scaffolds.q: Cones have LOWER sensitivity but HIGH resolution (sharp acuity).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Low convergence = each cone's signal is preserved = sharp edges.

scaffolds.q: Moving to bright light 'bleaches' rhodopsin, reducing rod sensitivity.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Light adaptation - rods become less responsive as rhodopsin is depleted.

scaffolds.q: In bright light, we lose the ability to detect very dim objects (sensitivity down).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Can't see stars at noon - rod sensitivity is gone.

scaffolds.q: In bright light, we gain sharp vision and color (acuity up).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Cone vision gives high resolution and full color perception.

scaffolds.q: Dark to light: Lose sensitivity (rods bleach), gain acuity (cones activate).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Trade the ability to see in dark for the ability to see detail in light.

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - moving to bright light loses sensitivity but gains acuity.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This is the rod-cone tradeoff during light adaptation. Rods = sensitive but fuzzy. Cones = need more light but sharp and colorful.
